gold.rationale: The article’s main conclusion is supported by the cited official rumor rebuttal and long-standing historical records identifying the monument as a Tang-era Buddhist carving. However, it is not fully precise because it broadens the media description beyond the source’s more specific claim about an AI-generated animation, and it includes a visually edited image that echoes the rumor’s framing.

atomic_points.post_summary: The post describes a viral claim on Chinese social media that the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great, and details how authorities, historians, and UNESCO reject this theory in favor of established historical records identifying it as a Tang-dynasty Buddhist monument.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A viral claim spreading on Chinese social media has cast the Leshan Giant Buddha in Sichuan as something other than what generations of records describe: not a Buddhist monument, according to the theory, but an original statue of the legendary flood-taming ruler Yu the Great that was later reshaped into a Buddha.
atoms: A viral claim is spreading on Chinese social media.
The claim concerns the Leshan Giant Buddha in Sichuan.
The claim asserts the Leshan Giant Buddha is not a Buddhist monument.
The claim asserts the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great.
The claim asserts the statue was later reshaped into a Buddha.
Yu the Great is described as a legendary flood-taming ruler.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The idea raced across posts, short videos and altered images that recast the famous cliffside face with a more secular, ruler-like look.
atoms: The claim spread via posts, short videos, and altered images.
The altered images recast the Leshan Giant Buddha with a secular, ruler-like look.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Authorities at the Leshan scenic area and Chinese rumor-control platforms have rejected the claim, saying it has no support in archaeology, inscriptions or the historical record.
atoms: Authorities at the Leshan scenic area rejected the claim.
Chinese rumor-control platforms rejected the claim.
Authorities stated the claim has no support in archaeology.
Authorities stated the claim has no support in inscriptions.
Authorities stated the claim has no support in the historical record.

sentence_level.sentence_text: After the theory drew wide attention online, officials linked to the Leshan Giant Buddha scenic area said it was built on internet speculation, not documentary evidence.
atoms: The theory drew wide attention online.
Officials linked to the Leshan Giant Buddha scenic area commented on the theory.
Officials stated the theory was built on internet speculation.
Officials stated the theory was not built on documentary evidence.

sentence_level.sentence_text: They said there are no excavation results or historical texts showing that the statue began as an image of Yu the Great.
atoms: Officials stated there are no excavation results showing the statue began as an image of Yu the Great.
Officials stated there are no historical texts showing the statue began as an image of Yu the Great.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Instead, they pointed to long-established records identifying the monument as a Tang-dynasty carving of Maitreya Buddha.
atoms: Officials pointed to long-established records.
The records identify the monument as a Tang-dynasty carving.
The records identify the monument as a carving of Maitreya Buddha.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That rebuttal has become the central official response as the claim continues to spread through reposted graphics and repackaged clips.
atoms: The official rebuttal is the central response to the claim.
The claim continues to spread through reposted graphics.
The claim continues to spread through repackaged clips.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The rumor was well suited to online circulation.
atoms: The rumor was well suited to online circulation.

sentence_level.sentence_text: It argued that the monument’s broad features, head shape and seated posture masked an earlier political or mythic figure that had supposedly been altered over time.
atoms: The rumor argued the monument's broad features masked an earlier figure.
The rumor argued the monument's head shape masked an earlier figure.
The rumor argued the monument's seated posture masked an earlier figure.
The rumor argued the earlier figure was political or mythic.
The rumor argued the figure had been altered over time.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Edited comparison images and an animated sequence gave the theory the appearance of visual analysis, not mere conjecture.
atoms: Edited comparison images were used to support the theory.
An animated sequence was used to support the theory.
These visuals gave the theory the appearance of visual analysis.

sentence_level.sentence_text: One widely shared comparison image, described online as a reconstruction, highlighted what looked like a crown-like outline and non-Buddhist facial contours to suggest a heroic figure beneath the current carving.
atoms: One widely shared comparison image was described online as a reconstruction.
The image highlighted a crown-like outline.
The image highlighted non-Buddhist facial contours.
The image suggested a heroic figure beneath the current carving.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Historians and local authorities rejected that interpretation, but the presentation proved compelling enough to draw millions of views and stir renewed debate.
atoms: Historians rejected the interpretation of the image.
Local authorities rejected the interpretation of the image.
The presentation drew millions of views.
The presentation stirred renewed debate.

sentence_level.sentence_text: What gave the claim unusual momentum was not any newly found inscription, excavation report or scholarly discovery.
atoms: The claim's momentum was not due to a newly found inscription.
The claim's momentum was not due to an excavation report.
The claim's momentum was not due to a scholarly discovery.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The strongest material behind it appears to have been an AI-enhanced visual sequence, along with altered animation and image edits that made an unsupported idea look more authoritative than it was.
atoms: The strongest material behind the claim was an AI-enhanced visual sequence.
Altered animation and image edits supported the claim.
These edits made the unsupported idea look more authoritative.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That difference matters.
atoms: The difference between visual polish and documentary history matters.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In disputes involving heritage sites, a polished visual can move faster than the slower work of documentary history, especially when it seems to reveal hidden layers in a monument people already know well.
atoms: Polished visuals can move faster than documentary history in heritage disputes.
This is especially true when visuals seem to reveal hidden layers in known monuments.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The documented history of the Leshan Giant Buddha is far less dramatic.
atoms: The documented history of the Leshan Giant Buddha is less dramatic than the viral claim.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Historical accounts place the start of construction in 713 during the Tang dynasty, when the monk Haitong is traditionally said to have begun the carving on the cliff face at Lingyun Mountain.
atoms: Historical accounts place the start of construction in 713.
Construction started during the Tang dynasty.
Monk Haitong is traditionally said to have begun the carving.
The carving is on the cliff face at Lingyun Mountain.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The project is tied to a Buddhist effort connected to the dangerous waters where local rivers meet, an area long known for strong currents and hazards to shipping.
atoms: The project is tied to a Buddhist effort.
The effort was connected to dangerous waters where local rivers meet.
The area is known for strong currents.
The area is known for hazards to shipping.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Haitong did not live to see the work finished.
atoms: Haitong did not live to see the work finished.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Construction later received support from regional officials, including senior Tang administrators who helped carry the project forward after his death.
atoms: Construction received support from regional officials.
Senior Tang administrators helped carry the project forward after Haitong's death.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The carving took about 90 years and was completed in 803, a timeline widely repeated in historical summaries of the site.
atoms: The carving took about 90 years.
The carving was completed in 803.
This timeline is widely repeated in historical summaries.

sentence_level.sentence_text: One important source is the stele known in English as the Record of the Great Maitreya Stone Statue at Lingyun Temple, Jiazhou.
atoms: An important source is a stele.
The stele is known in English as the Record of the Great Maitreya Stone Statue at Lingyun Temple, Jiazhou.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The inscription lays out the background of the monument, its sponsors and the progress of the work, and it identifies the statue as a Maitreya Buddha.
atoms: The inscription lays out the background of the monument.
The inscription lists the sponsors.
The inscription details the progress of the work.
The inscription identifies the statue as a Maitreya Buddha.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That point carries weight because the stele is not a modern reading imposed on the site centuries later.
atoms: The stele is not a modern reading imposed on the site centuries later.

sentence_level.sentence_text: It is a foundational historical record connected directly to the monument itself.
atoms: The stele is a foundational historical record.
The stele is connected directly to the monument.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Local gazetteers from Leshan tell the same story.
atoms: Local gazetteers from Leshan tell the same story as the stele.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Regional historical records describe the site as a Buddhist cliff carving and match the stele’s identification of the figure as Maitreya, not a repurposed secular ruler or mythic hero.
atoms: Regional historical records describe the site as a Buddhist cliff carving.
Regional records match the stele’s identification of the figure as Maitreya.
Regional records do not identify the figure as a repurposed secular ruler.
Regional records do not identify the figure as a mythic hero.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Taken together, those sources present a consistent documentary tradition: the Leshan Giant Buddha was conceived and built as a Buddhist image in the Tang period, not recut from an earlier statue of Yu the Great.
atoms: Sources present a consistent documentary tradition.
The Leshan Giant Buddha was conceived as a Buddhist image in the Tang period.
The Leshan Giant Buddha was built as a Buddhist image in the Tang period.
The Leshan Giant Buddha was not recut from an earlier statue of Yu the Great.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That record cuts against the most dramatic part of the viral theory.
atoms: The historical record contradicts the viral theory.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Yu the Great holds an important place in Chinese cultural memory as a sage ruler associated with flood control, so linking his name to a monument overlooking turbulent waters gives the claim an immediate intuitive pull.
atoms: Yu the Great is a sage ruler in Chinese cultural memory.
Yu the Great is associated with flood control.
Linking Yu the Great to the monument gives the claim intuitive pull.

sentence_level.sentence_text: But that is not the same as evidence.
atoms: Intuitive pull is not the same as evidence.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Officials responding to the rumor said there is no archaeological discovery, no surviving inscription and no reliable textual source showing that the giant carving was first made as Yu and only later turned into a Buddha.
atoms: Officials said there is no archaeological discovery supporting the Yu the Great theory.
Officials said there is no surviving inscription supporting the Yu the Great theory.
Officials said there is no reliable textual source supporting the Yu the Great theory.

sentence_level.sentence_text: UNESCO’s description of the site points in the same direction.
atoms: UNESCO’s description of the site supports the Buddhist origin theory.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The organization inscribed Mount Emei Scenic Area, including the Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area, on the World Heritage list in 1996.
atoms: UNESCO inscribed Mount Emei Scenic Area on the World Heritage list.
The Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area is included in the inscription.
The inscription occurred in 1996.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In its official materials, UNESCO treats the Leshan figure as part of a historic Buddhist monumental landscape and describes it as a major Buddhist monument carved into a cliff.
atoms: UNESCO treats the Leshan figure as part of a historic Buddhist monumental landscape.
UNESCO describes the Leshan figure as a major Buddhist monument.
UNESCO describes the Leshan figure as carved into a cliff.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That designation does not settle every historical argument, but it reflects the broad scholarly and heritage consensus on the site: a Buddhist stone colossus from the Tang era.
atoms: The UNESCO designation reflects broad scholarly consensus.
The consensus identifies the site as a Buddhist stone colossus from the Tang era.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The episode shows how quickly speculative claims about the past can gather force when they are paired with persuasive digital imagery.
atoms: Speculative claims can gather force when paired with persuasive digital imagery.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A dramatic animation or enhanced comparison image can create the impression of discovery where none exists, especially when viewers see it outside the context of inscriptions, local records and established scholarship.
atoms: Dramatic animations can create the impression of discovery where none exists.
Enhanced comparison images can create the impression of discovery where none exists.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In the case of the Leshan Giant Buddha, the documentary trail remains plain: the monument’s Buddhist origin is recorded, its construction history is well known, and the claim that it began as a statue of Yu the Great remains unsupported.
atoms: The Leshan Giant Buddha's Buddhist origin is recorded.
The Leshan Giant Buddha's construction history is well known.
The claim that the Leshan Giant Buddha began as a statue of Yu the Great remains unsupported.

paragraph_level: A viral theory on Chinese social media claims the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great, reshaped into a Buddha.
Authorities and rumor-control platforms reject the Yu the Great theory, citing lack of archaeological, inscriptional, or historical evidence.
The viral claim gained traction through AI-enhanced visuals and edited images that presented speculation as visual analysis.
Historical records, including the stele at Lingyun Temple, identify the monument as a Tang-dynasty carving of Maitreya Buddha.
Construction of the Leshan Giant Buddha began in 713 under monk Haitong and was completed in 803, taking about 90 years.
Local gazetteers and regional records consistently describe the site as a Buddhist monument, not a repurposed secular ruler.
UNESCO lists the site as a Buddhist monumental landscape, reflecting scholarly consensus on its Tang-era Buddhist origin.
The episode illustrates how persuasive digital imagery can spread unsupported historical claims faster than documentary evidence.

retrieved_evidence.text: Archaeology and history researchers in southwest China note that the carving of the Leshan Giant Buddha extended over several decades during the Tang dynasty. According to local gazetteers and temple records reviewed by specialists, the project is believed to have lasted around 90 years, continuing after the death of the initiating monk Haitong. Subsequent regional officials and patrons are described as providing resources and organisational support, helping to bring the monumental rock sculpture to completion in stages as conditions permitted.

Posts circulating on social media have featured reworked portrayals of the Leshan Giant Buddha that accentuate certain contours to suggest a heroic or legendary human figure, which some commentators loosely link to Yu the Great. According to cultural heritage officials and art historians, such speculative renderings do not align with the statue’s established iconography, which includes the seated posture, elongated earlobes and other standard Buddhist features. They emphasise that no archaeological reports, temple records or regional chronicles from Sichuan support the idea that the monument was ever dedicated to a non‑Buddhist figure before becoming known as a Maitreya carving.

According to cultural relics departments in Sichuan province, construction of the Leshan Giant Buddha is generally dated to 713 in the early Tang dynasty, with traditional accounts attributing the initiative to a monk named Haitong. Researchers say written records compiled by temples in the area, as well as later local histories, describe Haitong as advocating the project for many years and mobilising donations to fund the large-scale cliff carving overlooking the confluence of nearby rivers. These details are widely cited in current academic introductions to the site.

An online narrative circulating in recent days has claimed that the Leshan Giant Buddha in southwest China was originally a statue of the legendary ruler Yu the Great and was only later altered into a Buddhist image. According to people familiar with the discussion on major social platforms, the posts often present this idea as a bold “new interpretation” of the monument’s past, suggesting an earlier non‑religious function for the cliff sculpture before it allegedly became associated with Buddhism. The conjecture has been widely shared but remains presented largely without reference to primary historical sources, observers noted.

Chinese rumor-control offices and cultural authorities in Leshan have in recent days addressed online claims about the origins of the Leshan Giant Buddha, according to official statements. Scenic-area administrators said their review of existing research and archival material has found no evidentiary basis for the suggestion that the monument was first carved as an image of Yu the Great and later reworked. Officials indicated that the site will continue to be interpreted for visitors in line with established academic views, while relevant departments monitor the online discussion.

In recent reports about the viral online claims surrounding Yu the Great, the Leshan Giant Buddha in Sichuan is repeatedly described as a monumental Tang‑era cliff carving seated above the confluence of the Minjiang and other rivers. Scholars note that the statue, over seventy metres high and hewn directly into the red sandstone cliff, has long been identified in local gazetteers and Buddhist records as a representation of Maitreya. The monumental setting above the waterway, with the serene figure facing outward toward river traffic, is cited as consistent with its documented religious and protective functions dating back to the Tang dynasty.

The United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization inscribed the Mount Emei Scenic Area, including the Leshan Giant Buddha, on the World Heritage List in 1996, according to public records. In the organisation’s description, the area is presented as a major historic Buddhist landscape that combines religious architecture, natural scenery and long-standing pilgrimage traditions. The listing text characterises the Leshan monument specifically as a large seated Buddha carved into a cliff face, reflecting the spread and artistic expression of Buddhism in the region over many centuries.

Local chronicles and temple documents preserved in Leshan are broadly in line with the narrative given on the stele recording the Great Maitreya Stone Statue at Lingyun Temple, according to historians consulted this week. These materials, compiled over successive dynasties, refer to the cliff monument as a Buddhist image and link it to religious activities at the site. Researchers say the consistency among different textual sources from the region is one of the reasons current scholarship treats the statue’s Buddhist attribution as well established.

Media analysts observing recent online debates over the origins of the Leshan Giant Buddha note that some posts promoting the Yu the Great hypothesis have used highly stylised visual material. According to these observers, short videos and graphics have employed dynamic animation, dramatic transitions and reconstructed imagery to present the theory in an eye-catching format. Commentators say this presentation style may give an impression of expert backing or documentary-style authority, even though the underlying claim is not accompanied by verifiable primary-source references in most cases reviewed so far.

Heritage scholars this week drew renewed attention to a Tang-era inscription known as the Record of the Great Maitreya Stone Statue at Lingyun Temple in Jiazhou, which they say offers key information on the Leshan Giant Buddha. According to experts familiar with the text, the stele outlines the circumstances of the statue’s creation and explicitly identifies the cliff carving as a Maitreya image commissioned within a Buddhist context. Researchers argue that the document supports the long-standing view that the monument was planned as a religious work rather than adapted from an earlier, non-Buddhist sculpture.

According to museum curators and local authorities in Leshan, historical inscriptions, scenic‑area plaques and archival displays summarise the construction history of the Giant Buddha as a Tang‑era religious project initiated by the monk Haitong. Explanatory panels often reference documents like the “Inscription of the Great Maitreya Stone Statue of Lingyun Temple,” which records the statue’s dedication, sponsors and continuation under officials such as Zhangchou Jianqiong. These materials are frequently cited in academic publications and official statements as key evidence that the monument was conceived and venerated as a Maitreya Buddha, forming a central basis for refuting the viral narrative that links it to Yu the Great.

【“乐山大佛是大禹雕像改建”系AI动画生成】近日，“乐山大佛实际是大禹雕像，后被人为改成佛像”的说法在网络上流传，引发关注。对此，四川乐山大佛景区管委会辟谣称： 该说法无任何实证支撑，史料明确记载乐山大佛来源，相关传言仅是臆测。 《嘉州凌云寺大弥勒石像记》石碑清晰记录了乐山大佛的建造背景、发起者、资金筹集等关键细节，乐山地方志也有对应记载，二者相互印证，确凿证明大佛造像为弥勒佛。 乐山市官方史料记载，乐山大佛由海通禅师于唐开元元年（公元713年）发起开凿，因当时乐山三江交汇水域湍急，船毁人亡事故频发，海通禅师立志造佛以仰仗佛法减煞水势、永镇风涛，弥勒佛像因在武周时期广为流传成为造像首选。大佛建造历时90年，海通禅师圆寂后由剑南西川节度使章仇兼琼、韦皋先后捐俸续修，韦皋还主持开凿了《嘉州凌云寺大弥勒石像记》以记录造佛始末。1996年，峨眉山-乐山大佛被联合国教科文组织列入《世界文化与自然遗产名录》，名录中也明确其为唐代佛教摩崖造像遗存。（来源：四川互联网举报辟谣平台）

Close observations of the Leshan Giant Buddha’s head and upper torso reveal stylistic traits that specialists associate with Tang‑dynasty Buddhist sculpture, including the rounded facial structure, gently closed eyes and characteristic ushnisha‑like cranial form. Heritage experts note that the weathered surface of the sandstone cliff, the chisel marks and drainage channels around the head area reflect historical construction techniques used by artisans at Lingyun Temple. In online debates, researchers stress that these sculptural details corroborate textual sources identifying the figure as Maitreya, countering attempts to reinterpret the monument solely through imaginative comparisons with legendary rulers such as Yu the Great.

Historians and heritage experts consulted by local media this week reiterated that available sources consistently describe the Leshan Giant Buddha as a carving of Maitreya, a future Buddha figure in Buddhist tradition. According to these researchers, neither temple records nor regional chronicles mention the statue having served as a secular or mythological figure before its completion. They say current academic consensus holds that the monument was conceived and executed as a Buddhist project from the outset, with no documented evidence of redesign from an earlier non-religious statue.

The stele is known in English as the Record of the Great Maitreya Stone Statue at Lingyun Temple, Jiazhou.
The inscription lays out the background of the monument.
The inscription lists the sponsors.
The inscription details the progress of the work.
The inscription identifies the statue as a Maitreya Buddha.
The stele is not a modern reading imposed on the site centuries later.
The stele is a foundational historical record.
The stele is connected directly to the monument.
Local gazetteers from Leshan tell the same story as the stele.
Regional historical records describe the site as a Buddhist cliff carving.
Regional records match the stele’s identification of the figure as Maitreya.
Regional records do not identify the figure as a repurposed secular ruler.
Regional records do not identify the figure as a mythic hero.
Sources present a consistent documentary tradition.
The Leshan Giant Buddha was conceived as a Buddhist image in the Tang period.
The Leshan Giant Buddha was built as a Buddhist image in the Tang period.
The Leshan Giant Buddha was not recut from an earlier statue of Yu the Great.
The historical record contradicts the viral theory.
Yu the Great is a sage ruler in Chinese cultural memory.
Yu the Great is associated with flood control.
Linking Yu the Great to the monument gives the claim intuitive pull.
Intuitive pull is not the same as evidence.
Officials said there is no archaeological discovery supporting the Yu the Great theory.
Officials said there is no surviving inscription supporting the Yu the Great theory.
Officials said there is no reliable textual source supporting the Yu the Great theory.
UNESCO’s description of the site supports the Buddhist origin theory.
UNESCO inscribed Mount Emei Scenic Area on the World Heritage list.
The Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area is included in the inscription.
The inscription occurred in 1996.
UNESCO treats the Leshan figure as part of a historic Buddhist monumental landscape.
UNESCO describes the Leshan figure as a major Buddhist monument.
UNESCO describes the Leshan figure as carved into a cliff.
The UNESCO designation reflects broad scholarly consensus.
The consensus identifies the site as a Buddhist stone colossus from the Tang era.
Speculative claims can gather force when paired with persuasive digital imagery.
Dramatic animations can create the impression of discovery where none exists.
Enhanced comparison images can create the impression of discovery where none exists.
The Leshan Giant Buddha's Buddhist origin is recorded.
The Leshan Giant Buddha's construction history is well known.
The claim that the Leshan Giant Buddha began as a statue of Yu the Great remains unsupported.
The image depicts the Leshan Giant Buddha as a Buddhist monument carved into a cliff face.
A wide shot of the Leshan Giant Buddha carved into a red cliffside, with a river in the foreground and a tour boat visible.
The image is an example of the 'altered images' or 'reconstruction' used in the viral claim to suggest a crown-like outline and non-Buddhist features.
A close-up of the Buddha's head where the hair/crown area has been digitally altered or enhanced to look like a distinct, sharp crown, supporting the 'Yu the Great' theory.
The image shows the facial features of the statue, relevant to the discussion of 'broad features' and 'facial contours'.
A close-up profile view of the Leshan Giant Buddha's face, showing the nose, eye, and cheek in detail.
The image depicts visitors viewing historical records or inscriptions (the stele) that identify the statue as Maitreya Buddha.
Several people standing in a museum or exhibition hall, looking at wall panels that display text and images of the Leshan Giant Buddha and a stone stele.
Record of the Great Maitreya Stone Statue at Lingyun Temple, Jiazhou

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports the viral rumor, the official rebuttals, and the historical consensus, with all specific details (dates, names, UNESCO status) corroborated by the retrieved evidence.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The text is a faithful and well-sourced report of a debunked viral claim, aligning perfectly with official statements and historical records provided in the evidence.

images.scene_description: A wide-angle photograph showing the Leshan Giant Buddha carved into a red sandstone cliff face, with a river in the foreground and a tour boat passing by. The statue is surrounded by lush green vegetation.
images.explanation: Clean reference image of the monument.

images.scene_description: A close-up of the Buddha's head where a large, golden, crown-like structure has been digitally superimposed over the top of the head, replacing the natural rock and vegetation.
images.v2_evidence: A golden crown graphic is digitally overlaid on the statue's head, which is a clear visual edit not present in the physical monument.
images.explanation: The image is a manipulated version of the statue used to support the viral theory, clearly showing digital editing.

images.scene_description: A close-up detail shot of the Leshan Giant Buddha's face, focusing on the eyes and nose, showing the texture of the weathered stone.
images.explanation: Clean stock photo of the statue's face.

images.scene_description: Visitors standing in a museum or exhibition hall looking at informational panels on a wall. The panels contain text and images related to the Leshan Giant Buddha.
images.explanation: Clean photo of the museum exhibit.

image_analysis.global_notes: The post uses a mix of clean reference photos and one explicitly edited image (Image 2) to illustrate the viral claim. The editing in Image 2 is a clear V2 cue.

judge.level3_rationale: The post accurately debunks the viral claim that the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great, citing official and historical evidence that confirms its Buddhist origins; while one image is digitally edited to show a crown, the text correctly identifies this as part of the misleading viral content, so the overall message remains factually sound.
judge.reasoning_trace: The text is factually accurate and well-supported. Image 2 is edited (V2), but the text frames it correctly as a fake/altered image used by the rumor, so it doesn't mislead the reader about the truth. Therefore, L2 is just V2. L1 is Mostly True.
judge.core_claim: A viral theory claiming the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great is false and unsupported by historical evidence, which confirms it is a Tang-dynasty Buddhist monument.
key_subclaims: A viral claim on Chinese social media asserts the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great.
Authorities and historians reject the claim, citing lack of archaeological or textual evidence.
The claim gained traction through AI-enhanced visuals and edited images.
Historical records, including the Lingyun Temple stele, identify the statue as Maitreya Buddha.
Construction began in 713 under monk Haitong and was completed in 803.
UNESCO lists the site as a Buddhist monumental landscape.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: A viral claim on Chinese social media asserts the Leshan Giant Buddha was originally a statue of Yu the Great.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the circulation of this specific narrative online.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Authorities and historians reject the claim, citing lack of archaeological or textual evidence.
subclaim_findings.note: Official rebuttals explicitly deny the claim and cite lack of evidence.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The claim gained traction through AI-enhanced visuals and edited images.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence notes the use of stylised visuals and animations to promote the theory.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Historical records, including the Lingyun Temple stele, identify the statue as Maitreya Buddha.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the stele explicitly identifies the statue as Maitreya.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Construction began in 713 under monk Haitong and was completed in 803.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the 713 start date, Haitong's role, and the ~90 year duration.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: UNESCO lists the site as a Buddhist monumental landscape.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the 1996 UNESCO inscription as a Buddhist landscape.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post's core claim (that the viral theory is false and the Buddha is a Tang-dynasty monument) is fully supported by evidence. The text accurately reports the rumor, the official rebuttal, and the historical facts. The only distortion is in Image 2, which is an edited photo (V2) showing a crown overlay. However, the text explicitly identifies this as an 'altered image' used by the rumor-mongers, so the image serves as an accurate illustration of the phenomenon described, not a misleading representation of the truth. Since the text correctly contextualizes the edited image, there is no cross-modal inconsistency (C1/C2). With only one mild V2 label and no T1/T3, the verdict is Mostly True.

image_findings.explanation: Standard photo of the Leshan Giant Buddha, consistent with the text's subject.
The image shows a digitally superimposed crown on the Buddha's head, which matches the text's description of 'altered images' and 'AI-enhanced visual sequence' used to support the viral theory.
Close-up of the Buddha's face, consistent with the text's discussion of facial features.
Shows museum exhibits regarding the Buddha, consistent with the text's mention of historical records and official responses.
